title: Other Literature
intro: "Other Literature" spans literary traditions and cross-cutting bonus formats that fall outside the separately organized British, American, Russian, French, and German literature categories. Recurring answerlines include the archaic Greek lyric poet Sappho, the German epic-theater dramatist Bertolt Brecht, and English writers such as Oscar Wilde, Virginia Woolf, D. H. Lawrence, Jane Austen, Matthew Arnold, and Samuel Johnson, whose British and Irish tradition supplies the largest single cluster of authors in the corpus.

American literature forms a secondary cluster, running from Edgar Allan Poe's Gothic tales through Joan Didion's New Journalism to the contemporary satire of George Saunders, with the state of Florida recurring as a regional setting. Russian, German, and Scandinavian drama appear through Nikolai Gogol and Bertolt Brecht and through Henrik Ibsen's 1881 play Ghosts, while France is represented by the Renaissance satirist Francois Rabelais and by French-language literature more broadly.

A substantial share of answerlines are countries and languages rather than individual authors — China, French, Arabic, Ireland, Mexico, South Korea, and Yiddish — reflecting bonus questions that survey an entire national or linguistic tradition, such as Chinese literature via Lu Xun, South Asian and postcolonial writing via Salman Rushdie, Latin American letters via Mexico, and Jewish literature via Yiddish, rather than a single author or work. A smaller set of common-link and thematic answerlines — dogs, ghosts, letters, Hamlet, and even the composer Ludwig van Beethoven — connect works and figures across several of these traditions by a shared motif rather than by nationality or genre.

sections.name: Classical Antiquity
sections.blurb: Greek and Roman poets, playwrights, and epics that predate the modern national literary canons.

entries.note: Archaic Greek lyric poet from Lesbos, c. 630 BCE, famed for passionate verse and the Sapphic stanza.

sections.name: French Literature
sections.blurb: Writers and works from the French literary tradition, from Renaissance satire to the modern novel.

entries.note: Language of a literary tradition spanning Rabelais's satire, Voltaire's Enlightenment prose, and the modern French novel.
French Renaissance satirist who wrote Gargantua and Pantagruel, known for grotesque humor and the character Panurge.

sections.name: German and Scandinavian Literature
sections.blurb: German-language and Nordic playwrights spanning epic theater and Scandinavian Realist drama.

entries.note: German Marxist playwright who pioneered Epic Theatre's alienation effect; wrote Mother Courage and The Threepenny Opera.
Refers to Henrik Ibsen's 1881 Norwegian play Ghosts, a Realist drama about hereditary syphilis and inherited moral corruption.

sections.name: Russian and Eastern European Literature
sections.blurb: Russian and neighboring literary traditions, centered on nineteenth-century prose satire and drama.

entries.note: 19th-century Russian satirist; wrote the play The Government Inspector and novel Dead Souls, plus the story The Overcoat.

sections.name: British and Irish Literature
sections.blurb: Novelists, poets, and critics of England and Ireland, from the eighteenth century through Modernism.

entries.note: Irish aesthete and wit who wrote The Picture of Dorian Gray and The Importance of Being Earnest.
English Modernist and Bloomsbury Group member; wrote Mrs Dalloway and To the Lighthouse using stream-of-consciousness technique.
Shakespeare's tragic Danish prince, driven by his father's ghost to seek revenge on Claudius.
English novelist linked to Georgian poetry and Modernism; wrote Sons and Lovers, Women in Love, and Lady Chatterley's Lover.
Country whose literary tradition includes James Joyce, W. B. Yeats, Oscar Wilde, and Samuel Beckett.
English novelist of manners; wrote Pride and Prejudice, Sense and Sensibility, and Emma using free indirect discourse.
City frequently the setting for English literature bonuses, from Dickens's novels to Woolf's Mrs Dalloway.
Victorian poet-critic who wrote 'Dover Beach' and criticism like Culture and Anarchy; coined 'sweetness and light.'
18th-century English lexicographer and critic who compiled A Dictionary of the English Language; subject of Boswell's famous biography.

sections.name: American Literature
sections.blurb: United States writers spanning Gothic Romanticism, New Journalism, and contemporary satirical fiction.

entries.note: American Gothic writer who pioneered detective fiction; wrote The Tell-Tale Heart, The Raven, and other macabre tales.
Contemporary American short-story writer and satirist; wrote the Booker-winning novel Lincoln in the Bardo.
U.S. state frequently the setting or answer in bonuses on American regional literature, from Hurston to Hemingway's Key West years.
American essayist and novelist of the New Journalism movement; wrote Slouching Towards Bethlehem and The Year of Magical Thinking.

sections.name: Latin American Literature
sections.blurb: Literary traditions of Mexico and the wider Spanish-speaking Americas.

entries.note: Country whose literary tradition includes Octavio Paz, Juan Rulfo, and Carlos Fuentes among its major modern writers.

sections.name: Middle Eastern and Jewish Literary Traditions
sections.blurb: Arabic-language and Yiddish literature, including classical storytelling and Ashkenazi Jewish fiction.

entries.note: Language of the literary tradition behind One Thousand and One Nights and classical Islamic poetry.
Language of Ashkenazi Jewish literature, home to Isaac Bashevis Singer and Sholem Aleichem's Tevye stories.

sections.name: South and East Asian Literature
sections.blurb: Literary traditions of China, Korea, and South Asia, including modernist and postcolonial writers.

entries.note: Country whose literary tradition spans classical poetry, the Ming-era novel Journey to the West, and modern writer Lu Xun.
Father of modern Chinese literature; wrote 'Diary of a Madman' and 'The True Story of Ah Q.'
British-Indian novelist of Magical Realism; wrote Midnight's Children and The Satanic Verses, which prompted a fatwa against him.
Country whose literary tradition includes Han Kang's The Vegetarian and a wave of postwar Korean fiction.

sections.name: Literary Common Links and Motifs
sections.blurb: Cross-cutting bonus answerlines built around a shared theme, setting, or figure rather than a single tradition.

entries.note: Common-link answerline connecting literary dogs, such as Homer's Argos in the Odyssey and Buck in The Call of the Wild.
Common-link answerline for epistolary works composed of letters, such as Dracula, Les Liaisons Dangereuses, and The Color Purple.
German composer (1770-1827) bridging the Classical and Romantic eras in Vienna; wrote the opera Fidelio and the Ninth Symphony.
